text: The surgeon makes a four-centimeter incision around the belly button to enter the abdominal cavity, preparing for a limited right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon inserts a port on the right side of the abdomen, identifying the ink mark indicating the polyp location. The cecum and small intestine are visible with minimal adhesions. The transverse colon is reflected upwards, revealing the thin duodenum and the vessel feeding the right colon, which is tented up for dissection.

The surgeon begins by opening the peritoneum beneath the vessel using an articulating end seal. A small window is created under the mesentery, and the surgeon uses a hand-over-hand technique to maintain space, gently sweeping to lower the duodenum away from the dissection area. The duodenum and accompanying small vessels are visible as they are retracted downwards, revealing the retroperitoneal space. The peritoneum is then taken over the top of the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies a vessel with less surrounding tissue and prepares to ligate it, using standard technique.
The surgeon ligates the ileocolic artery, leaving a visible stump that pulsates slightly. The duodenum is positioned safely below the surgical field.

The surgeon lifts tissue adjacent to the duodenum, continuing dissection in the retroperitoneal plane. Using hand-over-hand technique, tension is applied to sweep down the retroperitoneal tissue, revealing the colon. The surgeon then focuses on mobilizing fibers on the lateral side, achieving medial mobilization.

The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel off the pelvic brim in the right lower quadrant using tension applied by the left hand. The ureter is visible running underneath the dissected area. Instruments are crossed due to the single-incision approach, while the right colon is being mobilized, revealing the undersurface of the retroperitoneum.
